speaker: interviewer
text: --five, an interview with Mrs. Hanna Lifschutz. Hanna, at the end of tape four you started to tell me about a German people coming to Zielonka while you were hiding by Roman Kierszniewski.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, so they came-- they approached-- I must tell you that during my stay at Kierszniewski. He worked for the underground party all the time. For the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Polish underground. They were wonderful people. Wonderful is not the word. And he worked. And he was worried that I had no papers whatsoever. So he asked them would they help me.

speaker: interviewer
text: They said they will help me if I occasionally help them. In which way? That if I take some parcels or letters or suitcase somewhere and don't ask any questions. Good. I was pleased to do something useful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they gave me a-- it was called a kennkarte. They gave me a document and a birth certificate, which was on the base of this birth certificate. They made me papers of a living person called Halina Kwiatkowska. This was a girl of my own age who lived in Warsaw in a completely different part. I didn't know about her. She didn't know about me. But the papers were made like that. Also, it was well known that if you had some money, a lot of money, there was a certain street. You could go there. You can buy. You could get a paper, similar paper like that. And I made out with the Romans that should, God forbid, somebody come and we should fall into the hands of the Germans-- and if they would hide a Jew, they could be shot outright-- that they should say that they don't know me at all. I just came to sell some butter, some milk, some something. And they are sorry for me so they allowed me to stay overnight.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were two German came. One was a volksdeutsche, which was a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] how would I say? A German national. Not a German. A Polish national of German origin, and a German official. And they were searching for Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they came to this village, to Roman. They saw the passport. They asked for my passport. And something in my attitude-- obviously I was frightened-- showed them that it's not quite right. And they inquired. I told them that I am smuggling milk and honey-- milk and-- not honey. Milk and butter. It was not as sweet as honey. Milk and butter. And I came to these nice people and I sold them. And they didn't pay me because they don't have money. So instead of paying me they allowed me to stay overnight. Sort of a story, you know, invented story. And then, because I was upstairs in the attic sleeping, they took the gun. They placed the gun against my back. And I was going forward because they were always afraid in case somebody there with guns. That if somebody should shoot, shoot straight into me. And I went with a gun in my back. I was walking up to the attic. And they inspected the attic. Being satisfied nobody's there, took me down, told me to take the coat, took my kennkarte. They're taking me to the police. I said goodbye to these people. I didn't say even goodbye. I say they're awful people. They didn't pay me. I was scared because they could have been shot. On the way they said, look, we are not going yet to Warsaw. They had a car. We have to inspect few houses on the way. We stepped into a house of a lady I knew before. She was almost a neighbor, a very nice, much nicer house that we were. Polish lady, Polish woman. And we went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: She opened the door. And she was very well composed. It was a very nice lady. And they said they want to search if there are no Jews. She said, Jews? Look, where are they? Who, Jews, she said. You must be mad. How could I keep Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: They inspected the house. In Polish houses there was always a cellar and a loft. But in the cellar they used to keep cabbage, and bacon, and coal, and beet root-- God knows what.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said to her, where's your cellar? She took us to the kitchen. Was a big opening. She opened the lid, and there were a few steps down. She went down the stairs. And she stretched her hand, went down to one of the walls, stretched her hands and she tell, look, look, look, look. The hands were completely outstretched. And she said, look, look, look, where are the Jews here? They looked here. They looked there. They didn't dare to go down. They looked, and looked, and looked. I looked as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Unwittingly nobody was there. They said, OK. They left. After the war, when I was already in India safe and sound, my friend Roman, with whom-- the family with whom I kept in touch from 1946 till today, with help and friendship. And we visited them now, and forever grateful, and so on. Told me that in this cellar were 15 Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the woman, indeed, was buying more food and created suspicion. And they were very sorry for me because I was taken. And they survived the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you see, the Poles occasionally here and there they were very noble and wonderful people. Yeah, Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was taken to Warsaw. And I was taken to a police station. And they wanted me to confess that I am a Jew. And I didn't. And probably in my destiny was that I should survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: I stayed overnight. I can't explain to you how that was. Who was there? Terrible looking people, women, prostitutes, or God only knows, thieves. I stayed overnight, in the day. Also they said to me, who can vouch for me? Where is my alibi? Who can say something that I am who I am? And I quoted this Professor Korzczewa because, I said, he's not a Jew. I used to come there is a dressmaker. What harm can it be to them? He came to the place. He saved my life. This is really wonderful people.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came and he said, I know this girl. She's teaching. She's teaching, she's smuggling. You can-- she's smuggling eggs and from the part of Germany, which was Reich. And she's not a Jew. They let--

speaker: interviewer
text: They said to myself, one woman, what? One girl, what's the gain? Probably. That's how I was saved.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I came to Warsaw. And I was allowed to stay. Roman was forever, forever noble and kind. So at late in the evening I was allowed to come to this-- was afraid-- till everybody's gone. Then he took me there. Freezing cold. He got tuberculosis from spraying the car. And then I must tell you another absolutely fascinating story.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I used to come in the evening and slept in one of the cars. He gave me additional cushions, you know. One car was bigger. One car was small. And I-- never not undressing, nowhere to have a bus.

speaker: interviewee
text: During the day I used to be on the stairs of this lady who gave me the shelter for two weeks. And after it she didn't want anymore. And I was just-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know, a beggar, and outcast, everything. So one of the nights I went into this car. And she always gave me some Ersatz coffee.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Roman, you know. And Irena, his wife, brought for me some sandwiches. Poor people themselves, but they-- you know, noble people, outstanding people. And I locked myself in the car and slept. And five o'clock in the morning I had to go out. Because at six already people were coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I found a place somewhere nearby in the burnt-out ruined house with one corner, a single room, where was one dilapidated, broken chair. So I used to go there, stay there for two, three hours, till it was light.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I went roaming on the streets. Sometimes somebody, Roman gave me an address to have a cup of tea. I had no money, nothing. Anyway, in one of the nights-- it was after a few weeks. Because I was there for quite a few months. Suddenly I was in the car and I have seen a face out of Hell. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] White face, red, burning black-- black burning eyes with completely red rims. Not human face.

speaker: interviewer
text: I screamed. I screamed. I screamed. Roman came. And he was very angry with this man. I think his name was Leon. He said, how dare you? How dare you come? I told you a million times you mustn't come. What happens? Before the war, this garage belonged to a very-- a Polish woman. Now, the husband was taken to the army, and probably died. And she was left a widow just before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was a very good accountant. This Leon was a very good accountant. And he had few-- many clients. And among his clients was this woman with this garage. And because she was quite helpless and so on, right, maybe she was pretty. God only knows. He managed her affairs in the garage. And it was a very good, lucrative place. When the war broke out, she was faced with a situation that he had to go to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. He was an Orthodox Jewish fellow. So she was heartbroken. What to do? She wanted to help him. She didn't know how.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they perceived an idea. They made a double ceiling in the garage. The double ceiling was normal. And then they made like a half-sliding ceiling so that he could lie on his stomach the whole day.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the night he could go down. And she used to bring him food. You see, another example. She could bring him food. And he could wash. He could go to the toilet, all kinds of things. But when I was there, Roman told him, don't go down because I bring this girl. And you go down when she's asleep. But he was curious who I was, and he peeped into the car. And I have seen him. From then on, I never slept. Because I was afraid to go to sleep in the car. And so we talked.

speaker: interviewer
text: With Leon?

speaker: interviewee
text: With Leon.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was quite amazing. He told me about his life. He was a very quite well-educated man. And about his family, and his friends, and how all is gone, and he was left alone. And during the uprising-- Polish uprising. Not Jewish, about which I will tell you. He joined the uprising and probably got killed or got lost because we never knew about him anymore. But it was extraordinary--

speaker: interviewer
text: Amazing. Amazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: He survived like that the whole war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were staying there after you met this man, Leon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So I was staying there. And I was desperate. And then one day I was trying to find, to remember, the name of our maid from our household from before the war. Maybe she could help me. Because I was completely helpless. I didn't absolutely know what to do. And I said, I vaguely remember that it was outside Warsaw. It was, you know, like a suburb. And I didn't have papers. I didn't look-- I was not washed. I was looking dreadful. And I went on a tram. And I said, I will simply go and try. And I never went into the tram because in case they use-- the Germans-- what the Germans used to do, they used to stop the car, the tram, and search for papers. And they didn't look for Jews anymore. They looked for young Polish people to take them to Germany to work for the war effort. And I remember once going in a tram on another occasion. I saw it few times. What they used to do, they used to take a number of Poles, young Poles, young or old. And they used to throw a kind of plaster of Paris into their mouths, preventing them from shouting. At they shot them at random. Shot them. They could shoot 15, 20 people at one time. Many streets like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on this particular day, I stood on [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the tram, on the entrance of the tram. You know, the-- before you enter the real wagon. On the [INAUDIBLE]. Just outside the sitting places, whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went into the corner, absorbed with my own thoughts, then lifted my eyes. And really, like a story of, like a probably Hollywood story, lo and behold, my cousin was there in front of me. He was, before the war, a very prominent architect. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was a very, very, very handsome man and not looking also not Jewish at all. His name was not Jewish. Rzedowski was a typical Polish name. Originally he helped me to get into this [INAUDIBLE]. Originally he helped me, which I didn't want to extend my talk to such a length. But I had met him there and he helped me to get the better quarters. And then I completely lost sight of him. He took his mother there. And then I lost sight of him because he joined the underground. He worked saving and helping Jews and non-Jews. And he lived somewhere with his mother. His two brothers went to Russia. And like that he was-- there was an organization which distributed money to Jews. I never got the money. Till then I didn't never knew about. And he said don't panic. Don't do anything that you recognize me. Go to the end of this route and step down and I will go. And I step down and then you tell me your story. I was, you know-- flabbergasted is not the word. I was overwhelmed completely. I nearly fainted. And I stepped down on the place where he-- the end of the route. And then he told me-- he didn't tell me about himself anything. He never did because he was afraid in case I am caught I might say. But he said, look, tell me everything about you. So I told him. And he said I'll help you with the new papers. It was like a fortune, you know. I couldn't dream about it. But because he worked for the underground, he could manage the finances. There was a source of-- because I was sent after the war-- it was called [? Felicia. ?] It was an organization that helped Jews and Christians, mostly Jews. Probably got the money from America or whatever. And he said, I meet you here in two weeks' time, on the same route, on the same place. And so we met in two weeks. Every two weeks I met him. And he said your papers are being made. It was difficult because what happened. They have to find a birth certificate of a dead person to be authentic. And the dead person had to be approximately of my age. And there were a lot of priests included in this plot. And there were the government-- the government in hiding. Not the government. The organization, the actual organization in hiding, helped a lot of people. And the movement, the resistance movement in Poland, was really very prominent. I mean, they speak of French marquees and things like that. But the Polish underground was fierce, and dedicated, and widespread. Anyway, meanwhile, one day-- I already had a little money, and my cousin gave me straight away an address to his friend, who was a safe person. That I could lodge there. I don't need to sleep on the stairs. I was sleeping on the stairs for weeks. And then at Roman's place at the garage. So I told Roman. I thanked him very much. And I went to this place. She was a beautiful woman, had a house. She was married to a non-Jew, to a Polish officer, who was skilled in cutting, which is very well-known story. All the Polish officers were killed by the Russians in cutting. And she was in this organization as well. And she give me shelter till my papers would come through. And as, you know, my luck would come, all along like it would be a certain break in my luck. I went one day to the place adjoining to the old ghetto, which was burnt out already and all that. And I was walking the street, and then I, you know, [INAUDIBLE] and think about what happened. And, you know, being complete like in a daze, it was the first time that I dared to go there, near that. And I didn't-- just opposite past the window-- it was a big, big, big, window, shop window. And I looked into this window, watching the things there. And a young girl approached, looked into this window. And then we looked at each other. And I mean, it is unbelievable to believe. But it really happened. And I met my friend Marsha Garfinkel, who was in the same school, a class higher.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's really unbelievable.

speaker: interviewee
text: It really happened. You see, it was like my providence was guiding things for me. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like my prov-- whatever it might be up there. I certainly am grateful. And she said, oh. She-- it was, you know, you can imagine the enthusiasm and the embracing and the outpouring of news and so on. Anyway, her whole family was saved. Her three sisters worked. They all-- because when the ghetto started, her cousin, to whom she was engaged, was a very clever man. He was an agriculture engineer. And he was very clever. He made for them Irish-- Christian-- Irish papers. All of them good, kennkartes. Proper papers that they were allowed to live in a house. They could be registered. They could find work and so on. They also didn't look particularly Jewish, all of them. And this was one man. His brother survived in another place. And he looked after four girls. Now, her name was at that time Marisha because Marsha wouldn't be, you know, the right name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she said to me, Stefan-- it was the name, Stefan. Stefan is alive, thank God, and he looks after all of us. Stefan had a little, modest place somewhere in Warsaw. And every Sunday he was visiting another girl pretending to be a boyfriend. And each girl had big thermos flask, putting food into this thermos flask, so it lasted him for the whole week. So she said she's working in a quite pleasant, quite nice, fashion shop. And they need somebody for stitching. They just need-- urgently, urgently, they need somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, look, you don't need to know much. I said, what? I said I have a good experience. And she can present me. I didn't know whether I'd be called Barbara, or Helena, or Marcia, or Marianne, or whatever. Because my cousin was in the process of making the papers for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she said no, you must come. She's a nice woman. She doesn't like Jews. But it doesn't matter. She doesn't know. Anyway, I told her this story about my cousin and all that. And she told me her story. And then she said, look, don't say anything at all. Just say that you-- this and that and other, nothing of truth. And she will engage you, and we'll be together. But, she said, you cannot do that unless Stefan agrees. Because it involves a certain degree of danger. That if we are together, two Jewish girls. So if Stefan says yes, then you can be. It was another, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Luck.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sort of human, not only kindness, but a noble gesture. And, of course, Stefan said yes. We even lived on Novagradska next to each other houses. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And her story is, of course, a big story. But it's too much to talk about it. Anyway, I began to work there. And there was also a very funny incident one day. Because the Germans forever and ever searched young people-- not Jews. But they searched young Polish people for work, as I told you, in motherland. In fatherland. In fatherland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they employed the same techniques. They came with a big lorrie. The German soldiers went out with their big boots, with their helmets, with their rifles, and the officers with their beautiful gear. And they didn't shout 'rouse.' But they went to every flat. And when they found a young man or young woman, rouse for work in Germany. Many Polish people went like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we watched. And she, the lady the owner of this mode, you know, the maison de mode, came in and she said, don't worry girls. You stay quietly here. I'm going down. She had a big flat downstairs where she kept materials and all kinds of things which were afterwards used for the fashion. She had a very good clientele. And I'm going down to see if any Jews are hiding in my premises. Not knowing that she has two Jews sitting up there. And Marisha and I, we look out of the window and the Germans are coming nearer and nearer. You know, if I didn't get a heart attack at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they're coming nearer and nearer. And we said what we'll we do? I don't have the papers. I will endanger them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, you know, completely out of my mind. And as they were coming to my house-- you truly may believe or not-- but like a providence, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with a sort of sword, shielding sword, [LAUGHS] sort of a healer's sword, or whatever, they stopped, boarded the train. They had enough people. Boarded the lorries, went off. And she came from downstairs, said no Jews are downstairs. It's quite all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's amazing, again. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, of course, we work there till about 11th of August. I remember the date because 11 of August was a beautiful May day. We were sitting in our-- we had a very nice premises. And we were sticking beautiful clothes for actresses and fabulous things in spite of the war. Because some of them collaborated. And then suddenly we heard sirens, very persistent and violent sirens. And the woman, you know, the lady of the house, of this owner, she came out. She said look, we all have to go to shelters. We are asked over the radio to go to shelters. So leave everything as it this. We go into shelters. It was the Warsaw uprising, not Jewish uprising.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not the Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was 11th August, 1943. And we went to the shelters. Occasionally we could go up then went to the shelters. It took-- the whole uprising-- the political situation was this. The Germans, who were very weak after the offensive on Stalingrad and in Russia. And they were already they knew they were losing the war. They were very frightened. And they were very weak. And half of the army perished in Russia. [INAUDIBLE]. And they were-- still they held a grip on Poland. But the Polish underground under the command of Bor Komorowski, General Bor Komorowski and other Polish high officers, who were constantly working in the underground. They decided because the Germans are weakening, and because the Russians are on the march to Vistula. Poland was divided into a suburb, Warsaw and Prague. That they are going-- they are absolutely on a march to Warsaw and to Berlin. And the Germans would be running away, and they are very much weakened. So the Polish people, in their pride, wanted to liberate Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're coming now to the end of tape five. Thank you.
